source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Cross-sectional AI valuations are deeply inconsistent — semi-cap equipment at 40x annualized earnings vs. DRAM at mid-single digit P/E cannot both be correct

tickers_audited.reasoning: Baker explicitly uses Nvidia's valuation as one of the two legs of the NVDA/GEV inconsistency argument — he specifically states NVDA appears cheap relative to its own history, making it a direct subject of this valuation inconsistency thesis.

Baker explicitly flags GE Vernova's valuation as implying power demand growth so extreme it would require an unfathomable loss of Nvidia GPU market share — it is a named, specific subject of the cross-sectional valuation inconsistency thesis.

Baker made a general claim about semi-cap equipment trading at ~40x annualized earnings as a sector-level observation. He did not specifically name ASML as the subject of this claim. Inferring ASML because it is the dominant semi-cap company is exactly the kind of sector-level inference the audit rules prohibit.

Baker's semi-cap equipment valuation argument was made at the sector level (~40x annualized earnings for semi-cap equipment broadly). There is no evidence in the summary that Baker specifically named KLAC in connection with this thesis — it is a sector inference, not a specific connection.

Same reasoning as KLAC — Baker's semi-cap equipment overvaluation argument was sector-level. LRCX was not specifically named as a subject of this thesis; including it is a sector-level inference that the audit rules prohibit.

Same reasoning as KLAC and LRCX — Baker made a sector-level claim about semi-cap equipment multiples without specifically naming AMAT. Removing per the strict standard that sector-level inferences do not qualify as specific connections.

Baker explicitly identifies Micron as the US-listed pure-play DRAM company and the actionable long expression of his DRAM thesis. This is a specific, named connection to the exact thesis — not a sector-level inference.
